items.question: An interviewer shows you three matrices with shapes A: 50 x 100, B: 100 x 20 and C: 20 x 10. Which of AB, BA, BC, CB and ABC are defined, and what are their shapes?
items.answer: AB is defined because 100 = 100 and has shape 50 x 20. BA is not defined because 20 != 50. BC is defined and has shape 100 x 10. CB is not defined because 10 != 100. The chain ABC is defined as (AB)C or A(BC); in both cases it has shape 50 x 10.

items.question: In a machine learning interview you are asked about the cost of multiplying two dense matrices. What is the time complexity of multiplying an m x n matrix by an n x p matrix using the standard algorithm, and why does the order of multiplication matter in a product of several matrices?
items.answer: The standard dense algorithm uses three nested loops and performs O(m * n * p) scalar multiplications for an m x n times n x p product. In a chain of several matrices, associativity allows you to choose the parenthesisation; different choices change the intermediate dimensions and therefore the values of m, n and p for each multiply, which can change the total cost by large factors even though the final product is mathematically the same.

items.question: In an algorithm design interview you are asked to relate matrix multiplication to linear transformations. How does matrix multiplication relate to composing linear transformations, and how would you explain this to a teammate debugging a sequence of transformations?
items.answer: Each matrix represents a linear transformation between vector spaces, and multiplying matrices corresponds to composing those transformations in order from right to left: if y = ABx, then x is transformed by B first and then by A. To explain this when debugging, you can think of each matrix as a box that turns inputs into outputs; the product AB is just wiring the output of the B box into the input of the A box, and checking shapes ensures that the output type of one matches the input type of the next.
